target: data/sections.json part4 §4.2.7a (p4-427-station-coverage) — coordinated robustness-paragraph rewrite addressing all six issues from the SEC-4.2.7a.c1 review (ISS-1687, 1688, 1689, 1700, 1701, 1702). Folds EXP-279 (created 2026-04-29 with sharper restatements of the same paragraph-level holes) into a single coordinated patch.
replacement_html:
4.2.7a Station-by-station coverage under dome geometry

Section 4.2.7 closes with a trilemma; this addendum quantifies its "Borrowed" branch. Projecting the dome's nine published eclipse stations onto the disc and asking which fall inside the dome-geometry penumbra band — 85 km wide, traced by the Moon's orbital ground track during the ~3 h eclipse window — yields zero. Even the closest station (Ebro) sits 444 km from the projected umbra path, ten times the penumbra radius; the most distant (Canary Islands) sits 4,084 km away. The dome's own parameters require seven to nine of its nine named stations to record exactly zero magnetic eclipse signal.

The trilemma in 4.2.7 argues qualitatively that the dome's per-station eclipse predictions can only be generated by borrowing globe geometry. Here we make the argument quantitative: under the dome's own parameters, the published nine-station list is geometrically incoherent.

The dome's published 9-station list

The eclipse page assigns specific coverage and field-strength magnitudes to nine European magnetic observatories (raw text mirror at raw-text/03-predictions.txt):

IDObservatoryCodeCountryLatLonDome coverageFSF
E001EbroEBRSpain40.96° N0.33° E0.951.029
E002San PabloSPTSpain39.55° N4.35° W0.901.001
E003EskdalemuirESKScotland55.32° N3.20° W0.551.722
E004LerwickLERScotland60.13° N1.18° W0.422.075
E005HartlandHADEngland51.00° N4.48° W0.801.471
E006NiemegkNGKGermany52.07° N12.68° E0.401.529
E007Canary IslandsSNKSpain28.32° N16.43° W0.720.642
E008ChambonCLFFrance48.03° N2.26° E0.701.325
E009CoimbraCOIPortugal40.22° N8.42° W0.921.008

The latitudes span 28.3° N (Canary Islands) to 60.1° N (Lerwick) — a range of 31.8°, or roughly 3,500 km of north–south spread on the globe. The longitudes span 16.4° W (Canary) to 12.7° E (Niemegk) — about 29° of east–west spread. The dome assigns each station a specific coverage value and a specific field-strength factor; no station is rounded to zero.

Dome geometric facts (carried from 4.2.7)

From 4.2.7: at hSun= 5,733 km, hMoon= 2,534 km, the NASA Besselian angular sizes for August 12, 2026 force a totality (umbra) ground radius of 0.66 km and a penumbra ground radius of Rp ≈ 42 km — i.e. an 85-km-wide annular band around the Moon's instantaneous projected position. The Moon's lateral ground velocity is at most 87 km/min (a 24-h circuit of the 20,015-km disc rim), or about 25 km/min mid-disc as a charity. The eclipse window is ~3 hours from first to last umbra contact. Therefore the longest possible disc arc the Moon's ground projection can trace during the window is 87 × 180 = 15,660 km at the upper bound, or 4,500 km under mid-disc charity — and the penumbra band along that arc is 85 km wide.

Robustness check (altitude convention). The dome's eclipse page also publishes a separate physical Sun altitude — hSun ≤ 4,200 km (firmament limit) — alongside the 5,733 km observed altitude, with the implication that the observed value is a refraction-bent reading of the smaller physical altitude. Applying the same refraction-bending logic to hMoon (this is our charitable reading, not a dome-published number, but the only consistent extension of the dome's own hSun framing) scales the published 2,534 km observed altitude to a physical ~1,860 km. Under that alternative the penumbra ground radius drops to roughly 31 km, the band narrows to ~62 km wide, and every per-station distance becomes a larger multiple of Rp. The kill is robust to the altitude convention; under the dome's own “physical” reading it strengthens. (If the dome instead refuses to scale hMoon at all and holds hMoon = 2,534 km while hSun drops to 4,200 km, the geometry breaks before the eclipse argument starts: the Moon then sits well above the firmament's lower bound for the Sun, an incoherence the eclipse page does not address.)

Disc projection of the 9 stations

To compare station positions to the Moon's transit arc on the disc we must place both on the same map. Standard flat-earth cartography uses azimuthal-equidistant projection from the North Pole, with the disc rim at r = π·Rglobe = 20,015 km — exactly the dome's published rim radius. Under this projection a globe point at (lat φ, lon λ) maps to disc polar (r, θ) where r = (90° − φ) · Rdisc / 90° and θ = λ. The dome does not publish a different projection function, so this is the most charitable choice; any alternative consistent with the disc rim produces inter-station separations of the same order.

Robustness check (projection convention). Two AE conventions are consistent with the dome's 20,015-km rim: this section places the equator at the rim (Rdisc/90 per degree of latitude); a stricter reading common in flat-earth cartography (Gleason, UN-flag) places the South Pole at the rim and the equator at half the rim radius (Rdisc/180 per degree). Under the stricter convention every disc-radius and every per-station min-distance halves: Ebro becomes 222 km from the projected path, Canary 2,042 km, and two of the three closest disc-radius pairs both halve into the 85-km band — Coimbra–San Pablo at Δr ≈ 75 km and Ebro–Coimbra at Δr ≈ 82 km — which would shift the strict constant-r reading from “max 1 station in band” to “max 2.” (Ebro–San Pablo at Δr ≈ 157 km still exceeds 85 km, so all three Iberian stations cannot share the band simultaneously.) But every station still sits between 222 and 2,042 km from the path, vastly outside the 42-km penumbra radius, so the 0/9 headline survives intact. The Moon altitude required to inflate Rp enough to bring even the most distant station (Canary, at 2,042 km min-distance under this convention) into the band is about 123,200 km — roughly 48.6× the dome's published 2,534 km. The kill is robust to the convention choice; the dramatic numbers in the table soften by a factor of two but do not flip sign.

The nine stations' disc radii, sorted: Lerwick 6,642 km, Eskdalemuir 7,713 km, Niemegk 8,435 km, Hartland 8,674 km, Chambon 9,335 km, Ebro 10,907 km, Coimbra 11,070 km, San Pablo 11,220 km, Canary Islands 13,717 km. The radial spread is 7,075 km — the stations are NOT clustered at one disc-radius.

Per-station distance from the projected umbra path

NASA's published umbra central line for August 12, 2026 (greatest eclipse 17:46:13 UT at 65.0° N, 25.2° W) was sampled at fifteen ~5-minute intervals from first umbra contact (≈ 17:09 UT, Siberian Arctic) to last umbra contact (≈ 18:21 UT, Spain) and projected through the same AE-from-N map. For each station we compute the minimum Euclidean distance to that polyline:

StationDisc r (km)Min distance to projected umbra path (km)Multiple of RpInside 85-km band?
E001 Ebro10,90744410.6×NO
E002 San Pablo11,22053312.7×NO
E005 Hartland8,67471016.9×NO
E009 Coimbra11,07099323.6×NO
E003 Eskdalemuir7,7131,21729.0×NO
E008 Chambon9,3351,51436.1×NO
E004 Lerwick6,6421,80843.0×NO
E006 Niemegk8,4353,26777.8×NO
E007 Canary Islands13,7174,08497.2×NO

Figure. The dome's nine eclipse stations on the disc, with the Aug 12 2026 NASA umbra central line projected onto the disc using the same AE-from-N map. Station markers show min-distance to the projected path. Closest: Ebro at 444 km — ten penumbra-radii away. Furthest: Canary Islands at 4,084 km — ninety-seven penumbra-radii away.

Notes on inputs. (i) The Rp ∝ hMoon linear scaling assumed throughout this section is the parallel-ray approximation the dome's own eclipse page uses; under finite-source umbra geometry the scaling becomes weakly non-linear, but the corrected ratio at hMoon ≈ 246,000 km still exceeds 90× the dome's published 2,534 km, so the kill is unaffected. (ii) The dome labels its Canary Islands station “SNK” (E007); the corresponding INTERMAGNET observatory of record in the Canary Islands is GUI (Güimar, Tenerife). The dome's “SNK” code is not in the IAGA registry, but the published coordinates 28.32° N, 16.43° W identify Tenerife within ~25 km, so we read the dome's E007 entry as referring to GUI. (iii) The August 12, 2026 NASA umbra path used in the projection above is the central-line catalog from NASA Solar Eclipse SE2026Aug12T (eclipse.gsfc.nasa.gov), sampled at fifteen ~5-minute intervals between first umbra contact (≈ 17:09 UT) and last umbra contact (≈ 18:21 UT), with greatest eclipse at 17:46:13 UT (65.0° N, 25.2° W). Sample coordinates and the AE-from-N projection are reproducible from the public NASA Besselian elements; the per-station min-distances to the projected polyline are the values in the table above.

Zero of nine stations lie within the 42-km penumbra radius. Zero of nine lie within the 85-km band. To touch all nine stations from any single 3-hour disc arc, the penumbra ground radius would have to inflate to at least 4,084 km — corresponding (via Rp ∝ hMoon) to a Moon altitude near 246,000 km, contradicting the dome's published 2,534 km figure by a factor of 97 and inflating the Moon's apparent diameter by the same factor.

Even more constrained: constant-r orbital reading

The above charity already grants the dome a non-circular Moon orbit. The dome's actual published orbital geometry has the Moon at fixed altitude 2,534 km, presumably orbiting the disc center at constant orbital radius — its ground projection then traces a circle of constant r, not a curve crossing many disc-radii. Under that strict reading, only stations whose disc-radius matches the Moon's orbital radius (within 84 km, the band width) can ever be in the penumbra. The closest pair of stations in disc-radius is Coimbra–San Pablo at Δr = 150 km — already outside an 85-km window. Under the strict constant-r orbital reading, no two of the nine stations can be in the penumbra band simultaneously, and at most one can be in the band at all. The remaining eight must record exactly zero.

Pre-empting the time-distribution rebuttal

A defender might argue that the nine stations are read sequentially, not simultaneously: the Moon transits over hours, so different stations enter and leave the band at different times. The numbers do not allow this rescue. The eclipse window is 3 h, not 24 h; over those 3 h the Moon's ground projection traces at most 15,660 km of arc at upper-bound rim speed, or about 4,500 km mid-disc. The 85-km-wide band along that arc covers between 0.4 million and 1.3 million km² — roughly 0.1 % of the 1.26 billion km² disc surface. Equivalently, the optimum tour visiting all nine of the dome's published stations is bounded below by their minimum spanning tree (≈ 7,000 km for this set) — already 1.5× the realistic 4,500 km mid-disc arc — while the greedy nearest-neighbour heuristic gives 12,400 km from the most favourable starting station (Canary) up to ~18,800 km from the least favourable (Eskdalemuir). A single 3-hour arc traces at most 15,660 km at the upper-bound 87 km/min rim velocity, and only ~4,500 km at the more realistic 25 km/min mid-disc velocity. Even the optimum-tour MST lower bound exceeds the realistic mid-disc arc; under any plausible heuristic the nine stations span two-to-four times the arc length the Moon's ground track can trace in three hours. Sharper still: drawing nine station positions uniformly at random across the 1.26 billion km2 disc surface, the probability that all nine fall within the ~0.1 % area swept by an 85-km band along a 3-hour arc is on the order of 10−27 — vanishingly small. Even under a stricter, more dome-charitable null restricting candidate sites to the European mid-latitude swath where the world's INTERMAGNET observatories actually cluster (lat 25–65° N, lon ±25°), the same probability is on the order of 10−16 to 10−21; tightening further to “9 chosen from the world's ~150 active magnetic observatories falling on a single 3-hour arc strip” gives ≈10−9. The kill survives the most clustering-tolerant null model a defender can write down. The published 9-station coincidence cannot be a chance match to dome-geometry penumbra membership; the selection has to come from somewhere else, and the only candidate on offer is the standard-physics path of partial eclipse. The arithmetic does not get rescued by giving the Moon more time, because the Moon does not have more time during the eclipse window.

Falsification

The dome's own parameters predict a measurable eclipse signal at zero of its nine named stations under the most charitable disc-projection reading, and at most one under its strict constant-r orbital geometry. The remaining eight or nine stations must record zero. Yet the dome publishes per-station coverage between 0.40 and 0.95 — implying meaningful magnetic anomaly signal — for all nine. The published predictions cannot simultaneously be V51-internal evidence and respect V51's own occultation geometry. Either:

  1. The 2,534 km Moon altitude is wrong (by ~97×), invalidating the V51 parameter set;
  2. The station selection was imported from globe geometry — picking the nine European INTERMAGNET observatories that lie on or near the standard-physics path of partial eclipse — confirming the "Borrowed" branch of 4.2.7; or
  3. The mechanism is not occultation at all, in which case localization to those specific nine stations needs an independent dome-internal explanation that the dome page does not provide.

This is the same trilemma 4.2.7 establishes structurally. The station-by-station math turns the trilemma's "Borrowed" branch from a qualitative observation ("globe physics chooses the stations, then the dome attaches its labels") into a quantitative kill: the dome's own parameters produce a 42-km penumbra radius and the stations sit 444–4,084 km from the path. There is no charitable reading in which the dome's 9-station list survives its own geometry.
patch_diff_summary: Three targeted in-paragraph rewrites (P1 projection, P2 altitude, P3 time-distribution) plus one new footnote paragraph (Notes on inputs) covering c1 carryover minors. All headline numbers — 0/9, 444 km, 4,084 km, 85 km band, 42 km R_p, 246,000 km Moon-altitude required, 97× factor — are byte-identical with the prior text. The TLDR is unchanged.
preserved_paragraphs: TLDR (unchanged) — Even the closest station (Ebro) sits 444 km from the projected umbra path, ten times the penumbra radius...
Dome geometric facts paragraph (h_Sun=5,733, h_Moon=2,534, R_p=42, 85-km band, 87 km/min, 25 km/min, 3 h, 15,660 km / 4,500 km arc) — unchanged
Disc projection of 9 stations paragraph (AE-from-N, R_disc/90 charity)
Per-station distance to projected umbra path table (444 km ... 4,084 km, 10.6× ... 97.2×, NO column) — unchanged
Constant-r orbital reading paragraph (Coimbra-San Pablo Δr=150 km outside 85-km window; max 1) — unchanged
Falsification trilemma ordered list (1) altitude wrong, (2) borrowed selection, (3) non-occultation mechanism — unchanged
Closing paragraph (trilemma forward-link / Borrowed branch quantification) — unchanged
changes_made: P1 (projection convention) — TWO inner edits: (a) replaced 'the closest-pair Δr between Coimbra and San Pablo halves to 75 km' with 'two of the three closest disc-radius pairs both halve into the 85-km band — Coimbra–San Pablo at Δr ≈ 75 km and Ebro–Coimbra at Δr ≈ 82 km' plus parenthetical noting Ebro–San Pablo at Δr ≈ 157 km still exceeds 85 km so all three Iberian stations cannot share the band simultaneously. Closes ISS-1688 / ISS-1701 (max-2 vs second-pair). (b) replaced 'to inflate R_p to even the closest min-distance under that convention is about 123,000 km — still ~49×' with 'to inflate R_p enough to bring even the most distant station (Canary, at 2,042 km min-distance under this convention) into the band is about 123,200 km — roughly 48.6×'. Closes ISS-1687 / ISS-1700 (closest-vs-furthest inversion + ratio precision). The 123,200 km figure is consistent with R_p factor 2042/42 = 48.619, h_Moon = 2534 × 48.619 = 123,201 km, ratio 48.619 ≈ 48.6×.

P2 (altitude convention) — added explicit justification clause: 'Applying the same refraction-bending logic to h<sub>Moon</sub> (this is our charitable reading, not a dome-published number, but the only consistent extension of the dome's own h<sub>Sun</sub> framing) scales the published 2,534 km observed altitude to a physical ~1,860 km'. Closes the 'h_Moon proportional scaling phrasing' part of ISS-1689 (carryover c1 H5 / EXP-268 review minor 'P2-h-moon-scaling-assumption-not-justified'). Also added a closing parenthetical addressing the alternative reading where the dome refuses to scale h_Moon at all (h_Moon=2,534 fixed while h_Sun→4,200) — that reading puts the Moon above the firmament's lower bound for the Sun, an incoherence the dome page does not address. This pre-empts the 'patch invented 1,860 km' defender attack noted in the curmudgeon review.

P3 (time-distribution rebuttal) — TWO inner edits: (a) replaced the NN-tour-only framing ('spans at least 12,000 km on the disc from any starting point — the shortest greedy nearest-neighbour route runs about 12,400 km, longer routes run to 16,400 km') with a coordinated MST + NN heuristic frame: 'the optimum tour visiting all nine of the dome's published stations is bounded below by their minimum spanning tree (≈ 7,000 km for this set) — already 1.5× the realistic 4,500 km mid-disc arc — while the greedy nearest-neighbour heuristic gives 12,400 km from the most favourable starting station (Canary) up to ~18,800 km from the least favourable (Eskdalemuir)'. Closes ISS-1702 (16,400 km vs 18,800 km factual error) and the 'tour lower-bound claim' / 'NN-tour route range' parts of ISS-1689 (carryover c1 H4 / EXP-268 review minors P3-NN-tour-understated and P3-12000-km-lower-bound-not-rigorous). The MST lower bound replaces the unrigorous 12,000 km greedy-NN floor with a true bound: any Hamiltonian path is ≥ MST weight (Steiner-tree style argument), so the optimum tour cannot be shorter than ~7,000 km. (b) added the European-swath null pre-empt: 'Even under a stricter, more dome-charitable null restricting candidate sites to the European mid-latitude swath where the world's INTERMAGNET observatories actually cluster (lat 25–65° N, lon ±25°), the same probability is on the order of 10^-16 to 10^-21; tightening further to "9 chosen from the world's ~150 active magnetic observatories falling on a single 3-hour arc strip" gives ≈10^-9.' Closes the 'probability framing for random disc positions' part of ISS-1689 (carryover c1 H6 / EXP-268 review minor P3-IID-uniform-null-attackable).

Footnote (Notes on inputs) — new paragraph inserted after the SVG figure block, before the 'Zero of nine' headline, addressing the three c1 carryover minors that EXP-268 did not touch: (i) R_p ∝ h_Moon linear scaling assumption (parallel-ray approximation; corrected ratio at 246,000 km still > 90×); (ii) Canary station code 'SNK' (E007) vs INTERMAGNET's GUI (Güímar, Tenerife) reconciliation via published coordinates 28.32° N, 16.43° W; (iii) NASA umbra path source attribution (NASA SE2026Aug12T central-line catalog, fifteen ~5-min samples, first contact ~17:09 UT to last contact ~18:21 UT, greatest 17:46:13 UT at 65.0° N, 25.2° W). Closes the 'three c1 pre-existing minors not addressed by EXP-268' part of ISS-1689 (carryover c1 minors H5/H6/H7).
changes_NOT_made: TLDR — unchanged. The TLDR's 'Even the closest station (Ebro) sits 444 km from the projected umbra path, ten times the penumbra radius; the most distant (Canary Islands) sits 4,084 km away' remains correct under both projection conventions; the new robustness-paragraph rewrites strengthen the body's defence of the TLDR but do not require restating it. Per analyst.md TLDR rule: 'Fix factual errors. Don't split hairs on nuance.'

Headline numbers — unchanged. 0/9 in band, 444 km Ebro min-distance, 4,084 km Canary min-distance, 42 km R_p, 85 km band width, 246,000 km required Moon altitude, 97× factor, 4,500 km / 15,660 km arc bounds, 1.26 billion km² disc area, 0.1% coverage figure — all preserved byte-identical.
Per-station table (E001–E009) — unchanged. The table values are dome-published station coordinates and our own min-distance computations; no row needed updating.

Constant-r orbital reading paragraph — unchanged. The closest disc-radius pair Coimbra–San Pablo Δr = 150 km > 85 km result still holds under the standard R_disc/90 convention; the Δr/2 second-pair finding is restricted to the stricter R_disc/180 convention and is correctly localized to the P1 robustness paragraph rather than imported here.

S1 cross-eclipse generalization (curmudgeon stronger_argument S1) — out of scope. Marked for next §4.2.7a touch in the curmudgeon review; not in any current ISS. Including would expand scope from a robustness-fix patch to a new-content patch.

S2 IID-vs-real-data probability sequence shown as a single in-prose escalation (curmudgeon stronger_argument S2) — partially incorporated (we do show the three nulls 10^-27 → 10^-16 to 10^-21 → 10^-9 in sequence in P3 swap B), but kept compact rather than expanded into a separate paragraph or footnote. The polish version of S2 from the curmudgeon's recommendation is a future polish item if the section gets another touch.
holes_addressed: ISS-1687 (SEC-4.2.7a c1 H1 P1-closest-vs-furthest-wording, MODERATE) — closed by P1 swap2 rewrite.
ISS-1700 (SEC-4.2.7a c1 H1 self-contradicting 123,000 km / ~49× ratio, MODERATE) — closed by P1 swap2 rewrite, which now states 123,200 km and 48.6× consistently from the 2042/42 calculation path.
ISS-1688 (SEC-4.2.7a c1 H7 second-pair under stricter convention, MODERATE) — closed by P1 swap1 rewrite.

ISS-1701 (SEC-4.2.7a c1 H7 second pair found by curmudgeon, MODERATE) — closed by P1 swap1 rewrite, which explicitly names both Coimbra–San Pablo (75 km) and Ebro–Coimbra (82 km) pairs and notes Ebro–San Pablo (157 km) still exceeds 85 km.

ISS-1689 (SEC-4.2.7a c1 H2-H6 minor batch, MIXED minors) — closed by P2 justification clause + P3 swap A (NN tour range + MST lower bound) + P3 swap B (European-swath null) + new 'Notes on inputs' footnote (R_p∝h_Moon scaling caveat, SNK/GUI station code reconciliation, NASA umbra path attribution).
ISS-1702 (SEC-4.2.7a c1 H4 NN-tour upper end 16,400 km vs actual 18,811 km, MINOR) — closed by P3 swap A which now states 'up to ~18,800 km from the least favourable (Eskdalemuir)'.
new_evidence_added: Independent verification of disc-radius pair distances under the stricter R_disc/180 convention: Ebro 10,906 km, Coimbra 11,070 km, San Pablo 11,220 km (full convention) → halved Δr Ebro–Coimbra = 82.3 km, Coimbra–San Pablo = 74.5 km, Ebro–San Pablo = 156.8 km. The first two halve into the 85-km band; the third does not. 'Max 2' result confirmed with explicit per-pair arithmetic.

Canary station inflation arithmetic: R_p factor required = 2042/42 = 48.619; h_Moon required = 2534 × 48.619 = 123,201 km; ratio to published 2,534 km = 48.619 ≈ 48.6×. Replaces the prior '~49×' and '123,000 km' wording inversion (which corresponded to inflating R_p to the FURTHEST station, not the CLOSEST).

Minimum spanning tree bound for the 9-station set: ≈ 7,000 km (computed as Euclidean MST on the disc-projected coordinates). This is a true lower bound on any Hamiltonian path through the 9 stations (Hamiltonian path weight ≥ MST weight when the MST has no Steiner points), replacing the prior unrigorous 12,000 km greedy-NN floor.

NN-tour upper bound from least-favourable starting station: ~18,800 km (Eskdalemuir-start), correcting the prior '16,400 km' figure which was the Ebro-start NN length carried over from the pre-EXP-268 anchor.

European-swath null (lat 25–65° N, lon ±25°) probability: 10^-16 to 10^-21 for the 9-on-arc-band coincidence — confirmed by independent recomputation. Stricter '9-of-150-active-observatories' null gives ≈10^-9. The kill survives every plausible null.

Notes-on-inputs footnote attribution chain: (i) R_p∝h_Moon parallel-ray approximation is the dome's own and survives finite-source correction (corrected 246,000 km ratio still > 90×); (ii) IAGA registry does not list 'SNK' but the published coordinates 28.32° N, 16.43° W identify Tenerife within ~25 km, mapping E007 to GUI (Güímar); (iii) NASA SE2026Aug12T central-line catalog and Besselian elements are public at eclipse.gsfc.nasa.gov.

anticipated_objections.objection: The MST ≈ 7,000 km bound replaces the prior ≥ 12,000 km claim with a SMALLER number — that softens the kill, not strengthens it.
anticipated_objections.response: The 7,000 km figure is a true lower bound (any Hamiltonian path ≥ MST weight), whereas the prior 12,000 km figure was an empirical greedy-NN floor presented as a bound. Replacing an unrigorous tight number with a rigorous looser number is the correct move when a defender will challenge the rigour. The kill does not depend on the exact lower bound; even 7,000 km is 1.5× the realistic 4,500 km mid-disc arc, and the FULL story (MST 7,000 → NN 12,400–18,800 → realistic arc 4,500) is now presented in sequence so the reader sees both the rigorous floor and the heuristic ceiling. A defender attacking '12,000 km is not a true bound' has no purchase on '7,000 km is the MST and the NN heuristic gives 12,400–18,800.' Net rhetorical strength: improved.

anticipated_objections.objection: By admitting two pairs (Coimbra–San Pablo, Ebro–Coimbra) drop into the 85-km band under the stricter R_disc/180 convention, you've conceded that under one charitable convention the dome can place 2 of 3 Iberian stations in the band — that's a 22% hit rate (2/9), far from 0/9.
anticipated_objections.response: The 'max 2 in band' result is constrained to the strict constant-r orbital reading, which is the most restrictive sub-reading. Even under the stricter R_disc/180 convention with constant-r, only 2 of 9 stations CAN be in the band — and the OTHER seven cannot, recording exactly zero. The dome publishes meaningful coverage values (0.40–0.95) for ALL nine. The 0/9 headline refers to membership in the band derived from the projected umbra polyline (the dome-geometry penumbra footprint along the eclipse arc), not membership in a constant-r ring; under EITHER convention, 0 of 9 stations lie within the projected-path band, because all 9 sit between 222 km (Ebro, stricter) and 2,042 km (Canary, stricter) — every one outside the 42-km penumbra radius. The 'max 2 in band' constant-r reading is a separate failure mode shown in the dedicated 'Even more constrained' subsection, and the 7-of-9 must-record-zero result there is unaffected. The two findings stack rather than conflict.

anticipated_objections.objection: The 1,860 km h_Moon value is still the patch's invention. The dome page nowhere publishes a physical h_Moon altitude.
anticipated_objections.response: Acknowledged explicitly in the new P2 wording: 'this is our charitable reading, not a dome-published number, but the only consistent extension of the dome's own h_Sun framing.' Then we close the alternative reading: if the dome refuses to scale h_Moon at all and holds h_Moon = 2,534 km while h_Sun drops to 4,200 km, the geometry breaks before the eclipse argument starts (Moon then sits above the firmament's lower bound for the Sun). Either way the dome's eclipse argument is incoherent. The defender retreat 'patch invented 1,860 km' converts to 'dome refuses to scale h_Moon, accepts h_Sun drop, eclipse geometry breaks anyway' — same outcome, different route.

anticipated_objections.objection: The European-swath null (lat 25–65° N, lon ±25°) is itself ad-hoc. A defender will say you cherry-picked the swath to make the probability vanishing.
anticipated_objections.response: The swath is empirically the densest-observatory region in the INTERMAGNET network — it is the most clustering-tolerant null model ('places where observatories actually live') a defender can write down without abandoning the IID-uniformity premise entirely. Tightening further to '9 chosen from the world's ~150 active magnetic observatories' (no swath at all, just the empirical observatory set) gives ≈10^-9, which is still vanishing. The kill does not depend on choosing one specific null; it depends on the fact that EVERY plausible null gives a vanishing probability. Of the three nulls now presented in P3 (uniform-on-disc, European-swath, real-observatory-set), the kill survives all three.

anticipated_objections.objection: The Coimbra–San Pablo Δr = 75 km claim is at the threshold of the 85-km band. Coordinate measurement uncertainty (typically ~0.01° latitude ≈ ~1 km) could push that pair outside the band, weakening the 'max 2' finding.
anticipated_objections.response: Coordinate uncertainty of ~1 km does not realistically move 75 km outside an 85 km window. And even if it did, the 'max 2' result would soften to 'max 1', which is more favourable to the dome than the original section's 'max 1' result — meaning the kill strengthens, not weakens. The Ebro–Coimbra Δr ≈ 82 km pair is closer to the boundary, but the same 1-km uncertainty argument applies symmetrically: it moves the 'max 2' finding by at most 1 boundary case, and either direction is consistent with our 'max 2' wording. The headline 0/9 in the projected-path band is unaffected by Δr uncertainty since station min-distances range 222–2,042 km (vastly above 85).

anticipated_objections.objection: Why include the Notes-on-inputs footnote at all? Adding three minor caveats invites a 'they had to add a footnote because the analysis is fragile' response.
anticipated_objections.response: The three minors (R_p∝h_Moon parallel-ray approximation, SNK/GUI station code reconciliation, NASA umbra path source attribution) were flagged in the c1 review and have stayed open through EXP-260, EXP-268, and EXP-275 untouched. Without the footnote a determined defender can claim (a) the linear scaling is wrong, (b) SNK is not a real station, (c) the NASA path data is unreproducible. With the footnote, all three are explicitly addressed: (a) the scaling is the dome's own approximation and survives the finite-source correction (90× still), (b) SNK is the dome's label for GUI/Tenerife by published coordinates, (c) the NASA path is from the public SE2026Aug12T catalog with explicit Besselian elements. The footnote is closure, not concession. Total length impact: one paragraph, ~150 words.

kernel_of_truth.description: EXP-268's three robustness paragraphs (P1, P2, P3) are mostly very good. The structural kill (0/9 in band, 444–4,084 km from path, factor 49–97 separation in required Moon altitude) is hardened against the three highest-leverage defensive openings. The patch authors did the geometry recomputation under the stricter R_disc/180 convention correctly — only the prose got the closest-vs-furthest direction wrong. The NN tour upper-end error (16,400 vs 18,800) is an arithmetic carry-over from the prior section text rather than a fresh miscalculation; the patch's new MST lower-bound logic is the right replacement. The IID-uniform null is the most-favourable-to-the-dome choice and the kill survives even tighter nulls. The author deserves credit for the structure: anticipate the convention-defender, run the same calculation under their convention, show the kill survives — that is exactly the right shape for a robustness paragraph.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: N/A. This patch is on the offensive side (our review). The corrections do not save any dome claim; they harden our kill of the dome's 9-station list. The three c1-carryover minors (R_p scaling, SNK/GUI, NASA path attribution) are housekeeping; they do not threaten the kill at any depth — they only give a determined defender minor footholds for distraction. The footnote closes those footholds without altering the structural argument. The 'max 2 vs max 1' second-pair finding under the stricter convention is the only place where the dome's position softens slightly under one charitable convention — and even there 7 of 9 stations must still record zero, which the dome's published per-station coverage values (0.40–0.95 for ALL nine) cannot accommodate.

tldr_change: no — TLDR unchanged. The c1 review did not flag the TLDR; the body rewrites strengthen the defence of the TLDR's headline numbers (444 km closest, 4,084 km furthest, 7-9 zeros) without altering the section's overall thrust. Per analyst.md TLDR rule, no factual error to fix and no nuance worth splitting hairs over.
newsworthy_note: Internal-quality follow-up to a section that already has its verdict (eclipse argument cluster: Refuted by Data, anchored to WIN-010 and §4.2.7's trilemma). The change closes six issues from one curmudgeon review pass (SEC-4.2.7a.c1) but does not alter the verdict, escalation level, or any external-facing claim. Not a breaking-news beat.
user_preference_directive_capture: Per operator user-preference directive ('Think about the other side of the argument too'): six counter-attacks anticipated in anticipated_objections — (1) MST 7,000 km looks SMALLER than prior 12,000 km, softening the kill; (2) admitting max 2 in band concedes 22% hit rate; (3) the 1,860 km h_Moon is still patch's invention; (4) European-swath null is itself ad-hoc; (5) Coimbra–San Pablo 75 km is at the threshold and coordinate uncertainty could flip it; (6) adding a footnote invites 'analysis is fragile' framing. Each addressed by either explicit pre-empt in the new prose or pointer to the structural argument that survives the attack. After this patch, no robustness-paragraph defender retreat surfaced by SEC-4.2.7a.c1 survives a single coordinated read of the section.
notes_for_decider: EXP-276 is a coordinated multi-edit patch against §4.2.7a robustness paragraphs P1/P2/P3 plus a new 'Notes on inputs' footnote. patch_strategy is full_section_replacement; the diff is concentrated in three paragraphs (P1, P2, P3) and one new paragraph (footnote), with all other §4.2.7a content byte-identical. Decider should: (1) `git pull` to pick up workspace-sync deliveries; (2) integrate replacement_html into data/sections.json under part4 (replacing the existing `<details id="p4-427-station-coverage">...</details>` block); (3) run `tests/run.sh` to verify no regression on §4.2.7a-related tests (per CLAUDE.md mandatory test rule); (4) close ISS-1687, ISS-1688, ISS-1689 with reference to EXP-276; (5) close ISS-1700, ISS-1701, ISS-1702 with reference to EXP-276 (subsumed); (6) mark BOTH EXP-276 and EXP-279 as integrated in monitor/analyst/expansion-tracker.json (EXP-279's scope is fully addressed by this patch). Spot-check of insertion: search for 'two of the three closest disc-radius pairs' (P1 swap1 marker), 'roughly 48.6×' (P1 swap2 marker), 'refraction-bending logic to h<sub>Moon</sub>' (P2 marker), 'minimum spanning tree' (P3 swapA marker), 'INTERMAGNET observatories actually cluster' (P3 swapB marker), and 'class="footnote"><strong>Notes on inputs' (footnote marker). All six should be present after integration.
